nativeRange.habitat: Sandy and gravelly-bottomed streams and rivers with clear, well-oxygenated water; often found in shallow marginal areas and among leaf litter near riparian vegetation

decorPreferences: fine sand substrate (essential — protects delicate barbels)
smooth rounded pebbles or river gravel
low to moderate planting (Java fern, Amazon sword, crypts)

diet.notes: Accepts sinking pellets, wafers, and granules as dietary staples. Eagerly takes frozen and freeze-dried foods including bloodworms, tubifex, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Also grazes on algae, biofilm, and organic detritus. Feed at or after lights-out to ensure the fish receive food before faster tankmates consume it. Never rely solely on scavenging — targeted feeding is required.

avoidWith: large boisterous fish that compete for bottom space
very large catfish that may outcompete for food

imageCandidates.notes: Check FishBase Corydoras metae species page for the current image; URL is illustrative — verify on fishbase.se/summary/Corydoras-metae.html
Wikimedia Commons image of Corydoras metae showing characteristic black bandit mask eye-stripe and lateral body stripe. Verify license and photographer attribution before use.

summary: A small, peaceful Colombian catfish instantly recognisable by the bold black ‘bandit mask’ stripe across its eyes and a matching lateral body stripe. Hardy, sociable, and an excellent community fish when kept in groups of six or more on fine sand. A 2024 taxonomic revision proposes reclassification as Hoplisoma metae.
careNotes: The Masked Corydoras (Corydoras metae) is endemic to the Meta River drainage in Colombia, where it inhabits clear, well-oxygenated streams with sandy or gravelly bottoms. Its trademark feature is a bold black stripe running horizontally across the eyes — giving rise to the common name ‘Bandit Cory’ — paired with a second black stripe running along the upper body from the dorsal fin to the caudal peduncle. Adults typically reach 4.5–5.0 cm, making them one of the smaller Corydoras species. A fine sand substrate (minimum 3 cm deep) is essential: like all corydoras, C. metae uses its sensitive barbels to forage across the bottom, and sharp gravel or coarse substrate quickly erodes these appendages, leading to bacterial infections and chronic stress. Smooth river pebbles or rounded gravel are acceptable accent decor but should not form the primary substrate layer. Keep in groups of at least six — Masked Corys are strongly social and individuals kept alone or in pairs exhibit stress behaviours including reduced feeding and erratic swimming. Water quality should be maintained at high standards: regular partial water changes (25–30% weekly) and efficient biological filtration are necessary. They prefer slightly soft, moderately acidic to neutral water mirroring their Colombian river origins, though captive-bred specimens tolerate a wider pH range than wild-caught fish. Temperature should be kept within 22–26°C; temperatures above 28°C reduce dissolved oxygen and stress the fish. Note that the pectoral-fin spines of Corydoras catfish carry a mild venom capable of causing painful puncture wounds to handlers — handle with care and use a soft mesh net rather than a hard rigid net. A 2024 phylogenetic revision by Dias and Britto proposes the reclassification of many Corydoras species, including C. metae, into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma; the new combination Hoplisoma metae is pending formal ICZN adoption as of 2026.
breedingNotes: Breeding Corydoras metae in captivity is achievable with some preparation. Condition a group of adults (ideally 2–3 males per female) on a high-protein diet of frozen bloodworms, brine shrimp, and quality sinking pellets. Spawning is typically triggered by a significant (4–6°C) water temperature drop combined with a large water change using cooler, slightly softer water, mimicking the onset of the rainy season. Courtship follows the characteristic Corydoras ‘T-position’ where the male fertilises eggs held in the female’s cupped ventral fins. The female deposits 3–6 adhesive eggs at a time on smooth surfaces — typically the aquarium glass, broad plant leaves, or flat stones. A single spawning session produces 20–80 eggs total. Remove eggs to a separate, shallow, well-oxygenated rearing vessel to protect from adult predation; a gentle air stone and a small amount of methylene blue reduces fungal losses. Eggs hatch in 4–5 days at 24°C. Fry are free-swimming after a further 24–48 hours and accept infusoria, micro-worms, and finely powdered fry food initially, graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii within a week.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Corydoras metae
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Meta River, Colombia), water parameters (temperature 22–26°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 36–215 ppm), adult size (~4.5 cm), substrate requirements (fine sand), social behaviour (shoaling, minimum group 6), diet, and breeding notes including T-position spawning behaviour and egg count.

additional.name: FishBase — Corydoras metae
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Callichthyidae, Siluriformes), native range (Meta River basin, Colombia), adult size (to 5.5 cm TL), temperature range (22–26°C), and diet (omnivore, benthic feeder). Also confirmed venomous pectoral-fin spines characteristic of Corydoras genus.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Corydoras Catfish Care Guide
additional.notes: Trade-level care guidance for Corydoras genus; confirmed minimum group size of 6, fine sand substrate requirement, omnivore diet including sinking pellets and frozen foods, easy care level, and compatibility with community fish. Specific C. metae parameters cross-checked against SeriouslyFish and FishBase.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Corydoras species profiles
additional.notes: Supplementary source for genus-level husbandry notes on substrate sensitivity (barbel erosion from sharp gravel), social requirements, and breeding triggers (cool water change replicating rainy season). Confirmed venomous pectoral spines and handler precautions for the Callichthyidae family.

additional.name: Dias & Britto (2024) — Hoplisoma reclassification
additional.notes: Reference for the 2024 taxonomic revision by Dias and Britto proposing reclassification of numerous Corydoras species (including C. metae) into the resurrected genus Hoplisoma. The combination Hoplisoma metae is proposed but pending broader adoption. Noted in careNotes and alsoKnownAs context.

fish.breedingNotes: Spawning triggered by cool water change (4–6°C drop) replicating rainy season; T-position fertilisation; 20–80 adhesive eggs per session deposited on glass or plant leaves. Remove eggs to rearing tank to prevent predation; hatch in 4–5 days. Fry accept infusoria, then baby brine shrimp nauplii.
